primary_diagnosis.name: Phenylketonuria with acute phenylalanine neurotoxicity (metabolic decompensation from dietary non-compliance)

pathophysiology_summary: Phenylketonuria is an autosomal-recessive deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase, so dietary phenylalanine cannot be metabolised to tyrosine and accumulates. When intact protein is consumed and the phenylalanine-free amino acid formula is stopped, blood phenylalanine rises to neurotoxic levels: it competes with other large neutral amino acids for transport across the blood-brain barrier, impairing neurotransmitter (dopamine, serotonin) and myelin synthesis and directly injuring the developing brain. Acutely this produces hyperactivity, severe irritability, insomnia, tremor, dystonic posturing and hyperreflexia; alternative metabolism to phenylketones (phenylacetate) produces the characteristic musty/mousy body and breath odour. Reduced tyrosine limits melanin synthesis, giving hypopigmentation (fair skin, blonde hair). Sustained high phenylalanine causes irreversible demyelination and intellectual decline, which is why rapid reduction is time-critical. Treatment is to eliminate all intact protein and resume the phenylalanine-free amino acid formula to drive metabolic clearance, under specialist metabolic supervision with phenylalanine-level monitoring.

differential.diagnosis: Acute phenylalanine neurotoxicity (metabolic decompensation of PKU)
differential.evidence_basis: Known PKU with an acute behavioural and new neurological decline — the highest-priority explanation in a patient with this inborn error.
Agitation, hyperactivity, insomnia and tremor in an adolescent can suggest stimulant use and must be considered.

differential.diagnosis: Primary bipolar mania / acute psychiatric episode
differential.evidence_basis: Severe agitation and insomnia can look like a manic episode; the trap is to anchor on a primary psychiatric cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the metabolic cause should lead given the known diagnosis, but stimulant use and primary psychiatric illness are legitimate considerations to screen.

differential.diagnosis: Acute phenylalanine neurotoxicity (metabolic decompensation of PKU)
differential.evidence_basis: Two weeks of stopping the formula and eating normal protein, plus the musty odour and new tremor, tie the crisis directly to the metabolic disorder.
Sudden onset over hours with hyperreflexia and a systemic metabolic context is not a benign chronic tremor.
Still worth a screen, but the pathognomonic musty odour and documented dietary non-compliance make it far less likely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute phenylalanine neurotoxicity (metabolic decompensation of PKU)
differential.evidence_basis: Musty odour + new extrapyramidal signs + hyperreflexia + sustained non-compliance is a coherent metabolic-neurotoxicity picture.

differential.diagnosis: Primary bipolar mania / acute psychiatric episode
differential.evidence_basis: The tremor, dystonia, hyperreflexia and musty odour are not features of a primary psychiatric illness — the neurometabolic driver explains the whole picture.
Confirmed on the constellation and history; requires urgent phenylalanine measurement and metabolic team management to reverse and prevent irreversible injury.

red_flags.description: New continuous bilateral resting tremor with intermittent dystonic hand posturing (new extrapyramidal signs)
red_flags.clinical_significance: New extrapyramidal signs mark acute CNS phenylalanine neurotoxicity; they can progress to seizures and irreversible injury if levels are not reduced.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to convulsions, worsening encephalopathy and permanent neurological damage.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective CNS irritability from the metabolic neurotoxicity; a clinician-only finding not obtainable via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of the severity of neurotoxic involvement.

red_flags.description: 48 hours of escalating agitation, hyperactivity and total insomnia
red_flags.clinical_significance: An evolving neurotoxic encephalopathy; the escalating trajectory signals an emergency in progress.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in urgent metabolic intervention while the toxin continues to accumulate.

red_flags.description: Roughly two weeks of dietary non-compliance with refusal of the phenylalanine-free formula and intake of normal protein
red_flags.clinical_significance: The direct cause of the toxic phenylalanine accumulation; identifies the reversible driver.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to instruct the metabolic washout that actually treats the crisis.

red_flags.description: Strong musty / mousy odour of breath and skin
red_flags.clinical_significance: A clinical marker of markedly elevated phenylalanine (phenylketone excretion) — near-pathognomonic in this context.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Loss of a key clue pointing away from a primary psychiatric or drug cause.

red_flags.description: Emergency neurological features — seizure, inability to hold objects from severe tremor, altered consciousness, or violence — not yet present
red_flags.clinical_significance: These are the life-threatening endpoints of untreated neurotoxicity; their appearance mandates immediate 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If they develop and are not escalated to 000, risk of status epilepticus, injury or death.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the crisis to his intellectual disability or 'challenging behaviour' and missing the acute, reversible metabolic driver — the central safety trap in disability medicine.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on a primary psychiatric diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading the agitation and insomnia as a manic/psychiatric episode and treating on that basis, ignoring the neurometabolic cause.
Assuming stimulant intoxication in an agitated adolescent and over-ordering a drug screen while delaying metabolic assessment.
Dismissing new bilateral tremor as essential/benign tremor despite its acute onset, hyperreflexia and metabolic context.
Attempting remote reassurance or waiting for a routine review when urgent in-person metabolic measurement and management are required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Serum phenylalanine level — the definitive marker of severity
Formal neurological examination for dystonia and extrapyramidal signs
Blood pressure and heart rate without a home device
Degree of encephalopathy / formal cognitive assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an acute metabolic decompensation of a known inborn error of metabolism; arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment by the metabolic team or emergency department for phenylalanine measurement and supervised management; instruct immediate resumption of the patient's own prescribed phenylalanine-free amino acid formula and complete avoidance of intact protein while transfer is arranged; escalate to 000 if a seizure, inability to hold objects, altered consciousness or violence develops. Do NOT prescribe or direct sedation remotely to an adolescent.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The musty odour plus known PKU plus new neurological signs make the metabolic cause recognisable; the real risk is diagnostic overshadowing (writing it off as behaviour, psychiatric illness or drugs) or under-action — remote reassurance when urgent in-person metabolic care and bloods are required.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism (ASIEM) — dietary management of phenylketonuria
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute elevation of phenylalanine from dietary non-compliance requires urgent reinstitution of the phenylalanine-free amino acid formula, elimination of intact protein, and specialist metabolic team review with phenylalanine-level monitoring.
